one_line_takeaway: Adversarial perturbations crafted on a CNN surrogate (VGG16) transfer to classical HOG-feature classifiers (KNN, SVMs, Decision Tree, shallow ANN) with 16.6%-59.1% relative accuracy degradation -- comparable to or worse than neural-to-neural transfer -- refuting the assumption that hand-crafted feature engineering is an inherent defense, and revealing that FGSM (not PGD) is the more effective transfer attack against these non-differentiable pipelines in 100% of tested configurations.
key_finding: The protective hypothesis (that HOG's gradient quantization/spatial binning filters adversarial signal) is strongly refuted: all classical/ANN classifiers suffer substantial accuracy degradation under transferred FGSM at eps=4/255, with relative drops ranging from 16.6% to 59.1% depending on configuration and classifier, and this is comparable to or exceeds the neural-to-neural VGG->AlexNet transfer baseline (14.4% relative drop under FGSM). At the best HOG configuration (C5, block size=3), relative drops were 30.7% (Kernel SVM, 0.862->0.598) and 16.6% (KNN, 0.716->0.597), versus AlexNet's 14.4% (0.90->0.77). A second, unexpected finding is 'attack hierarchy reversal': across all 32 classical-ML configuration-model pairs (and 40 total including ANN), FGSM caused strictly larger accuracy drops than PGD in 100% of cases -- the opposite of the standard neural-network pattern where iterative PGD dominates single-step FGSM. The authors hypothesize PGD overfits to surrogate-specific high-level features that do not survive HOG's gradient-based encoding, while FGSM's single-step perturbation corrupts the low-level edge structures HOG preserves. HOG block-size (cells-per-block) shows the strongest mitigating correlation with FGSM robustness (r=0.60 for accuracy, r=0.41 for retention) and epsilon shows the strongest negative correlation with FGSM retention (r=-0.65); cell size and orientation-bin count show negligible correlation (|r|<0.15). Increasing block size from 1 to 3 raised KSVM FGSM retention from 42% to 69%, but even the best configuration retained only partial robustness (17-31% relative degradation persisted). Doubling the perturbation budget from eps=4/255 to eps=8/255 increased relative degradation by up to 30 percentage points with no observed saturation.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (Section III-IV, not the related-work survey in Section II) is the first systematic empirical study of adversarial transferability FROM a CNN surrogate (VGG16, fine-tuned on CIFAR-10, 98% train / 89% test accuracy) TO classical feature-engineered classifiers built on Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features. FGSM and PGD (10 iterations, step 2/255) L-infinity adversarial examples are crafted on VGG16 at two budgets (eps=4/255 and eps=8/255) and transferred, without any retraining, to four classical models (KNN k=3, Decision Tree depth=10, PCA(0.95)+SGD Linear SVM, PCA(0.80)+RBF Kernel SVM with C=1) plus a shallow ANN (256-64-16-10), each trained only on clean HOG features across eight systematically varied HOG configurations (cell size, orientation bins, cells-per-block). A separate VGG->AlexNet neural-to-neural transfer experiment serves as the paper's own baseline for comparison. The authors explicitly distinguish their contribution from three cited prior works (Siddique et al. on lateralized HOG+SIFT defenses, Mohammed et al. on direct attacks on HOG pipelines, Lal et al. on HOG+feature-fusion defenses with adversarial training) by noting none of those studies isolates HOG-alone robustness against cross-paradigm (neural-to-classical) transfer -- this isolation is the paper's novel angle.
threat_model: Black-box, cross-paradigm transfer attack: an adversary crafts L-infinity-bounded perturbations (FGSM single-step or PGD 10-step, eps in {4/255, 8/255}) using gradients from a CNN surrogate (VGG16) that has no relationship to the deployed target system's architecture. The target systems are classical ML pipelines (KNN, Decision Tree, Linear SVM, Kernel SVM, shallow ANN) operating on hand-engineered HOG features, which the attacker is assumed not to have gradient access to (HOG feature extraction is not end-to-end differentiable). The paper frames this as relevant to security-critical deployments -- resource-constrained, legacy, or interpretability-critical systems -- that continue to rely on classical/HOG pipelines and might be assumed immune to neural-network-derived adversarial examples because of the non-differentiable feature-engineering bottleneck.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section V-H, 'Threats to Validity and Future Work'): (1) External validity -- single dataset (CIFAR-10) and single surrogate architecture (VGG16); results not yet validated on higher-resolution datasets (e.g., ImageNet) or diverse surrogates (ResNet, ViT). (2) Statistical rigor -- no repeated trials with different random seeds and no confidence intervals reported, due to hardware/time constraints. (3) Surrogate diversity -- only VGG16 tested as attack surrogate. (4) Metric selection -- accuracy is the sole evaluation metric; per-class precision/recall/F1 and confusion-matrix analysis are left for future work. (5) No defenses explored -- the study characterizes vulnerability but does not test mitigations such as input preprocessing (JPEG compression, spatial smoothing) or adversarial training on HOG features.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (60,000 images, 32x32x3, 10 classes, 50k train/10k test, upsampled to 64x64)
quantitative_results: VGG16 surrogate: 98% train accuracy, 89% test accuracy on CIFAR-10; under its own FGSM/PGD attacks, VGG accuracy collapses to ~0.18 (FGSM) and ~0.01 (PGD)
AlexNet (neural-to-neural transfer target): 90% train / 84% test clean accuracy; 0.77 under FGSM transfer (~13-14% relative drop) and 0.83 under PGD transfer (~7% relative drop)
Classical ML relative accuracy drops under FGSM at eps=4/255 range from 16.6% to 59.1% depending on classifier/HOG configuration

Best HOG configuration C5 (block size=3): KSVM 0.862 clean -> 0.598 FGSM (30.7% relative drop); KNN 0.716 clean -> 0.597 FGSM (16.6% relative drop); ANN 0.648 clean -> 0.452 FGSM (30.3% relative drop)
FGSM causes larger accuracy drops than PGD in 32/32 classical-ML configuration-model pairs (100%) and in 40/40 pairs including ANN
Doubling epsilon from 4/255 (C1) to 8/255 (C8) increases FGSM relative drop e.g. KSVM from 58.3% to 82.3%, KNN from 44.1% to 74.4%, LSVM from 34.8% to 56.9%, DT from 41.0% to 57.7%
Increasing HOG block size from 1 to 3 improves FGSM accuracy: KNN 0.304->0.597 (+97%), RBF-SVM/KSVM 0.310->0.598 (+93%)

Pearson correlations across 32 classifier-configuration pairs: block size vs FGSM accuracy r=0.60, block size vs FGSM retention r=0.41, epsilon vs FGSM retention r=-0.65, cell size and orientation bins |r|<0.15
Cosine similarity between clean and adversarial images at eps=8/255 (worst case): mean 0.8311 (FGSM), 0.8321 (PGD), confirming perceptual imperceptibility is largely preserved
C5 (block=3) achieves highest FGSM retention across classifiers (69-83%); C8 (eps=8) produces lowest (18-43%)
Grid-search cross-validation accuracies on clean HOG (config C1): KNN 0.269, Decision Tree 0.234, Linear SVM 0.413, Kernel SVM 0.334
baselines_compared: VGG16-BN (attack surrogate, on-surrogate accuracy under own attacks)
AlexNet (neural-to-neural transfer target, establishing the paper's own baseline for cross-paradigm comparison)
Four classical classifiers: KNN (k=3), Decision Tree (max_depth=10), Linear SVM (PCA 0.95 + SGD), Kernel SVM (PCA 0.80 + RBF, C=1)
Shallow ANN on HOG features (256-64-16-10 architecture)
